content.problemStatement:
The following is an easy game that the setter of this problem played when he was 8:

A boatman, a wolf, a sheep, and a cabbage are on the bank of a river. They have a small boat that is capable of carrying the boatman and at most one other animal/item with him. However, if left alone by the boatman, the wolf can eat the sheep, and the sheep can eat the cabbage. How can all four be moved safely to the opposite bank of the river?

Here is a nice visualization of the whole process in the original game. Disclaimer: writers, testers and CodeChef are not related to this link.

This leads to a more general problem. If there are other groups of animals/items with the boatman, **and if the boat is big enough to seat at most **K** animals/items other than the boatman**, how quickly can all of them be moved to the opposite bank of the river in such a way that nobody/nothing gets eaten?

We will give you the number of animals/items (not including the boatman). We will also give you the new capacity of the boat. Moreover, we will give you all a list of pairs of the form "**X Y**" where the **X**-th animal/item will be eaten by the **Y**-th one if they are both on the opposite bank to the boatman.

Assuming a valid solution exists, you are to determine the **minimum number of boat trips** across the river to achieve the task.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains three integers N, M, and K, where N is the number of animals/items not including the boatman, M is the number of relations of the form 'X will be eaten by Y', and K is the capacity of the boat (not including the boatman). The following M lines contain pairs of the form X Y with the meaning that the X-th animal/item will be eaten by the Y-th one if they are both on the opposite bank to the boatman.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimum number of boat trips across the river necessary.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first test case is the original version of the problem. The boatman needs to make 7 trips to move all animals and items to the other bank safely. The second test case is a simpler version of the problem, where the boatman needs to make 3 trips.
The boatman needs to make 5 trips to move all animals and items to the other bank safely.
The boatman needs to make 3 trips to move all animals and items to the other bank safely.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm to explore all possible states of the system. Each state represents the current position of the boatman and the animals/items on both banks of the river. We start with the initial state where all animals/items are on the same bank as the boatman. Then, we generate all possible next states by moving the boatman and at most K animals/items to the other bank. We keep track of the number of boat trips made so far and stop when we reach a state where all animals/items are on the opposite bank and no animal/item is in danger of being eaten. The minimum number of boat trips is the answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(2^N * M * K) per test case, where N is the number of animals/items, M is the number of relations, and K is the capacity of the boat. The total time complexity is O(T * 2^N * M * K), where T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(2^N) for storing all possible states of the system.
